text: The surgical team prepares for a transperitoneal approach using a robotic system with two assistant ports. They reflect the colon and small bowel medially, retract the ureter and gonadal vein laterally, and follow the psoas muscle to reach the main renal hilum. Vessel loops are placed around the main renal artery and vein, and the kidney is circumferentially mobilized.

The surgeon frees the ureter from the kidney and circumferentially mobilizes it, rotating the kidney 180 degrees to bring the posterior surface anterior and the upper pole to the lower pole. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to score the tumor, followed by clamping the main renal artery and vein. Dissection is carried out to locate the posterior branch entering the tumor, proceeding through the scored resection area.
The surgeon checks the bipolar device and prepares a vessel loop, gently retracting tissue to expose blood vessels for further dissection.
The surgeon places a clip around a vein near the adrenal area and proceeds to mobilize the kidney, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.

The surgeon visually identifies the right kidney, colon, duodenum, vena cava, and retracted liver. The renal vein and artery are exposed, along with the ureter and right adrenal gland. The kidney is freed from surrounding structures.

The surgeon has freed up the kidney and is rotating it, ensuring the ureter is disconnected from the lower pole to facilitate movement. The kidney is flipped, revealing the upper and lower poles and the posterior surface. The ureter is visible, along with a posterior branch of the renal artery. An ultrasound is requested to score the area of the tumor.
The surgeon uses ultrasound imaging to identify the tumor within the kidney, marking areas with and without tumor presence. The remainder of the kidney appears clean and free of tumor.

The surgeon uses ultrasound to identify and score the tumor on the kidney, marking the boundaries of the tumor area. The tumor is visible on the screen, and the scoring is completed up to a specific point where the tumor is no longer present.

The surgeon prepares to start the partial nephrectomy, considering the dissection of an artery that is in close contact with the tumor and supplies it. The ultrasound is removed from the surgical field.

The surgeon uses gentle suction to clear the surgical field, revealing the area where the kidney needs to be excised. An artery is visible entering the kidney, but it is not being addressed at this moment. The focus is on dissecting the vessels associated with the intrarenal part of the tumor.

The surgeon applies gentle suction to the surgical field before placing a bulldog clamp on the main renal artery and vein to prevent back bleeding. The procedure involves addressing a central hilar tumor, necessitating careful management of the renal vein during dissection.
The surgical team opens the surgical field wider, preparing to clamp the renal vein. The surgeon discusses optional clamping on the left side of the renal vein and notes the ischemia time.
The surgeon navigates the instrument straight towards the tumor, identifying a branch of the artery that may need to be taken. The approach is direct and precise, focusing on the tumor's location.
The surgeon identifies the tumor mass and attempts to release it from surrounding tissues, making precise incisions to separate the tumor from adjacent structures.

The surgical field reveals the central sinus of the kidney with no visible parenchyma. Fibrotic tissue is observed covering the tumor, and a potential arterial branch is identified, which may need to be taken to facilitate operation with the fourth arm.

The surgeon navigates around the collecting system, using a ureteral catheter while avoiding taking the entire artery. The clip will be removed later, and adjustments are made to avoid instrument clashing due to the absence of bariatric ports.

The surgeon applies a wet clip to the tissue, then removes the scissors before proceeding. The visual field shows a distinction between fat and tumor tissue, with a noted capsular breach. The collecting system is visible, and the surgeon adjusts the approach from the opposite side while managing the left arm's position.
The surgeon's left arm is visibly non-functional during the procedure. The camera focuses on an area of concern, revealing a large mass of tumor tissue.
The surgeon identifies a small tumor thrombus within the vein and clears it using suction, while noting the proximity to an artery and the risk of devascularizing the kidney.

The surgeon evaluates an artery that is directly supplying the tumor and decides to clip it, aiming to remove the tumor with adequate margins. The use of Firefly imaging is mentioned as potentially beneficial for assessing vascularity during this step.

The surgeon identifies the thrombus and prepares to anchor it with stitches using a needle driver and V-lock suture. The vein is unclamped as the tumor excision progresses, with visible attention to timing and precision.

The surgeon prepares to unclamp the vein, observing for any bleeding that may occur, and plans to suture any bleeding sites. The needle driver is used, although it is noted that a suture cutting needle driver is unavailable.

The surgeon unclamps the kidney, observing its pink coloration indicating restored blood flow. Hemostasis is achieved through strategic placement of clips on larger intrarenal vessels, minimizing bleeding. The surgeon notes that only fifteen percent of the kidney has been sutured, yet effective hemostasis is maintained. Each wet clip will be undersown to prevent migration into the collecting system.

The surgeon requests a cut and observes the kidney, noting that one part appears pink while another does not. A stitch is placed, and the surgeon requests a 3-0 suture and suction. Irrigation is applied to the surgical field. The surgeon expresses concern about suturing potentially affecting underlying blood vessels, emphasizing the importance of achieving hemostasis.

The surgeon identifies the main spot for intervention and requests the removal of a wet clip. A team member is instructed to pull the urethral catheter down by five centimeters, ensuring proper positioning.
The surgeon closes off the collecting system using available sutures, while irrigation is applied to the surgical field. The irrigation appears light, and the presence of bleeding is noted.

The surgeon observes the lower pole of the kidney, noting its unhappy appearance compared to the healthier front. A visible line of demarcation is present. The surgeon decides against further suturing due to potential complications and opts to use a bolster for support. There is minor venous oozing, but the kidney shows areas of pinkness, indicating adequate blood flow.

The surgeon places multiple bolster stitches, using a two-zero suture for substantial compression. Suction is applied to remove clots from the surgical field. The needle tip is positioned low to ensure effective compression with the bolster. Flow seal is prepared for use.

The surgeon takes substantial bites with the needle to prevent cutting through the tissue, using the left hand for better angle control. The collecting system is closed, and the bolster is applied to ensure no leakage. Suction is used, followed by the application of flow seal.

The surgeon points out the thrombus adjacent to the tumor, which is in contact with the central sinus. The intact capsule of the kidney is visible, along with the collecting system on the tumor. The parenchyma loss is limited to the area directly above the tumor, with the rest of the kidney sculpted effectively.

The surgeon uses absorbable clips to secure the area around the tumor, following a strategy of scoring the tumor with ultrasound and deepening the circumferential margin of resection. The approach involves moving from the hilum towards the kidney, following the main renal artery, and selectively taking branches only when necessary. Wet clips are used to minimize suturing, with stitches placed only in specific areas.

The surgeon observes the line of demarcation on the kidney, noting that it has shrunk. The tissue appears pink, indicating good perfusion, even at the lower pole. The kidney is flipped back to potentially enhance perfusion.

The surgeon applies a wet clip and carefully pulls the stitch vertically up to avoid tearing the tissue. Another wet clip is placed, ensuring the stitch is tight enough to be effective but not so tight that it cuts through the tissue. The surgeon checks for hemostasis and advises against removing multiple needles simultaneously to prevent losing them.

The surgeon flips the kidney over to inspect the opposite side, revealing a pink and healthy appearance of the tissue. Suction is applied to clear the field, and the blue adhesive is visible on the surface. The camera is cleaned to ensure a clear view of the surgical area.

The surgeon assesses the vascularity of the kidney, noting that the lower pole appears healthy with good perfusion, while a posterior area shows a blue discoloration. The anterior aspect of the kidney looks satisfactory. A nephropexy stitch is placed to secure the kidney, with emphasis on using two stitches to prevent torquing and potential infarction.
